does: This paper introduces AlphaDPO, an adaptive preference optimization algorithm that improves upon Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) by replacing fixed reward margins with dynamic, adaptive ones. The method employs an adaptive preference distribution that balances contributions from the policy model and reference model to compute personalized reward margins for each training example. The authors provide theoretical guarantees demonstrating that AlphaDPO effectively serves as a surrogate for the underlying optimization objective, and show through experiments that this adaptive approach achieves better alignment between language models and human preferences compared to fixed-margin alternatives.
connects: This work builds directly on the family of offline preference optimization methods that emerged as computationally efficient alternatives to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). It specifically extends Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and Simple Preference Optimization (SimPO), which reformulated reward learning by reparameterizing the reward function to avoid explicit reward model training. The paper identifies and addresses two key limitations in these prior methods: DPO's dependence on a potentially suboptimal reference model and SimPO's inflexible assumption of uniform reward margins across diverse preference datasets. AlphaDPO contributes to ongoing efforts to simplify and stabilize LLM alignment while maintaining theoretical soundness.
enables: By introducing adaptive margin selection, this work opens pathways for more flexible and data-driven preference optimization strategies in LLM alignment. The adaptive approach could enable better performance on heterogeneous preference datasets where different examples benefit from different margin specifications, potentially improving sample efficiency and convergence stability. Future work could explore how adaptive margins interact with diverse data distributions, extend the method to multi-objective alignment scenarios, and investigate whether similar adaptive principles apply to other components of preference optimization. The theoretical framework may also inspire adaptive techniques for other alignment methods beyond preference optimization.
